ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for wonton noodle soup; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific wonton noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for wonton noodle soup as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of wonton noodle soup weighing approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, broth, and 4–6 pork and vegetable wontons.

nutrient_source.title: Wonton noodle soup, prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO INFOODS SEA composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Din Tai Fung vegetable and pork wonton noodle soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for wonton soup (NDB 06932), wheat noodles (NDB 20110), and pork-vegetable filling composites, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Chinese-style noodle soups. Per-100 g values reflect the full bowl (broth + noodles + wontons) at approximately 450 g serving weight. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and wonton soup — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable and pork wonton noodle soup. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles (GI ~47–54, mean ~52, low category) and wonton soup analogues. The dish is predominantly wheat noodles in broth with a modest wonton component; GI is dominated by the noodle fraction. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as (52 × 44.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.9, rounded to 23.0. Atkinson et al. 2021 confirms wheat noodle GI range 47–55.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g bowl provides approximately 1710 mg sodium (~74% of the 2300 mg daily limit), making this dish a significant sodium load for individuals with hypertension; portion reduction, requesting low-sodium broth, or limiting soy sauce additions is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low) but a glycemic load of ~23 per bowl due to the substantial noodle portion, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and consider pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork filling and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per serving) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle Soup with Vegetable and Pork Wontons (菜肉馄饨面, Cài Ròu Húntún Miàn) is a classic Chinese soup noodle dish consisting of thin wheat noodles served in a clear, savoury pork or chicken bone broth, accompanied by handmade wontons filled with a mixture of minced pork and Chinese cabbage (napa cabbage). The wontons are wrapped in thin wheat-flour skins and boiled until tender, yielding a delicate, silky texture with a juicy, well-seasoned filling. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, ginger, and spring onion, imparting a light but umami-rich flavour. This dish is a staple of Chinese culinary tradition, widely enjoyed across mainland China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and throughout Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it is a popular restaurant offering. At Din Tai Fung, the wontons are handcrafted to exacting standards. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~21.6 g per 450 g bowl) from the pork filling and noodles, moderate carbohydrates (~44 g) primarily from wheat noodles, and is relatively low in fat (~10.8 g). The sodium content is high (~1710 mg per bowl) due to the seasoned broth, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~23) owing to the noodle volume, making portion awareness important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for this specific dish (Din Tai Fung vegetable and pork wonton noodle soup) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy wonton soup and wheat noodle entries cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle values. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 06932 wonton soup; NDB 20110 wheat noodles) and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data; no exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry was identified for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this dish type. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar dishes. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries (GI 47–54); no direct measurement exists for this specific dish. 7. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual weight may vary by restaurant and portion. 8. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 9. is_halal is set to false as the dish contains pork. 10. food_group corrected from input value 'Vegetables' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite noodle soup dish containing noodles, meat, and vegetables.
